Title: Habitat-adapted microbial communities mediate Sphagnum peatmoss resilience to warming

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Heat shock protein 70 family molecular chaperones
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Metatranscriptomics/RNA-seq differential expression with microbiome transfer and temperature treatments; host growth and chlorophyll fluorescence phenotyping
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Warm-origin microbiome induces HSP70 transcript accumulation, including under ambient conditions, consistent with thermal preconditioning and enhanced warming resilience
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Ambient versus warming chamber treatment; transfer of ambient-origin versus warm-origin microbiomes
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced under warming and by warm-origin microbiome
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: 15 HSP70 members induced under warming; 33 of 35 detected HSPs induced in plants receiving warm microbiome at ambient temperature
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: 16S/ITS amplicon sequencing BioProject PRJNA644113; metagenome and metatranscriptome BioProject PRJNA644538
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Warm-origin microbiome induced host heat shock response, especially HSP70 family transcripts. 2. HSP70 induction occurred even without experimental warming, indicating microbiome-driven thermal preconditioning. 3. This response was associated with improved moss growth and photosynthetic performance under warming.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Family-level result; individual HSP70 gene IDs were not reported. HSP70 was treated as a host stress-response marker rather than a directly manipulated causal gene.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential marker pathway for microbiome-induced thermotolerance, but not directly validated for breeding or engineering in this study.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Allene oxide intermediate derived from fatty acid oxidation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes allene oxide to form 12-oxo-phytodienoic acid (OPDA), an intermediate/signaling compound in jasmonic acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms pathway intermediate leading to jasmonic acid
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Metatranscriptomics/RNA-seq ontology enrichment and differential expression associated with microbiome transfer and temperature treatment
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Abiotic and biotic stress response; implicated in thermotolerance
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Warm-origin microbiome elicited host genes contributing to jasmonic acid synthesis via AOC, consistent with microbial induction of stress-response metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Warm-origin microbiome transfer; ambient versus warming temperature conditions
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced in plants receiving warm-origin microbiome
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Jasmonic acid synthesis ontology enriched under warm-microbiome influence
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Warm-origin microbiome induced jasmonic acid synthesis-related host expression via AOC. 2. AOC links microbiome treatment to OPDA/jasmonate pathway activation. 3. Jasmonate-related enrichment was proposed as part of microbial thermal preconditioning but was not directly functionally tested.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed explicitly as enzyme contributing to jasmonic acid synthesis; no individual gene ID or direct metabolite measurement reported. Enrichment disappeared after warming exposure, so contribution may reflect preconditioning/defense rather than sustained output.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Prior literature cited in paper notes AOC overexpression can reduce growth in Marchantia polymorpha, but this was not tested here.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate pathway node for manipulating microbiome-responsive thermotolerance, but causal engineering value not established in this study.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for phenolic secondary metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Metatranscriptomics/RNA-seq ontology enrichment associated with microbiome transfer and temperature treatment
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Warm-origin microbiome induced expression of phenolic biosynthesis enzymes including PAL, associated with host stress acclimation responses
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Warm-origin microbiome transfer; ambient versus warming temperature conditions
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Phenolic secondary metabolite production ontology enriched in host transcriptome
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Host phenolic metabolism was transcriptionally induced by microbiome and temperature context. 2. PAL was specifically mentioned among enzymes induced by warm microbiome. 3. This response was linked to potential thermal preconditioning or defense, not directly to measured metabolite accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PAL was not directly manipulated or biochemically assayed; evidence is transcriptomic and pathway-level.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for enhancing stress-associated phenolic metabolism, but causal utility was not directly demonstrated.
